aliases: Ginger Sauce Chicken Rice (Com Ga Kho Gung)

english: Ginger Sauce Chicken Rice (Com Ga Kho Gung)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Vietnamese ginger braised chicken rice; closest match is the generic chicken rice dish class FOODON_00002187.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Com Ga Kho Gung; A0CKM (chicken dish with rice) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for this Vietnamese-Singaporean dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of ginger sauce chicken rice as served in a Singaporean hawker centre typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a mound of steamed white rice topped with braised ginger chicken pieces and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Ginger Sauce Chicken Rice (Com Ga Kho Gung) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ginger Sauce Chicken Rice (Com Ga Kho Gung), cr_id G00010532, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — steamed white rice analogous entry
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Com Ga Kho Gung as a composite dish. GI estimated at 68 based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white jasmine rice (GI ~68–72 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The braised chicken and ginger sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to modestly reduce the overall GI relative to plain rice due to protein and fat co-ingestion. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 52.9) / 100 ≈ 35.4, using 350 g serving size and 15.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg per 100 g from fish sauce and soy sauce in the braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce during preparation, avoid adding condiments at the table, and limit portion size to control total sodium intake, noting that a 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,134 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with GI ~68 and 15.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g drives postprandial glucose elevation at typical serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit the rice portion to no more than half a cup cooked, consider substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic impact, and request reduced sugar in the braising sauce to lower overall glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 324 mg per 100 g combined with cholesterol 13 mg per 100 g and moderate fat 4.0 g per 100 g; ginger provides cardioprotective gingerols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken to reduce saturated fat, request low-sodium seasoning, and benefit from the anti-inflammatory and lipid-modulating properties of ginger, while keeping total portion size to 300 g or less to manage sodium and caloric load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger (gingerols) provides clinically demonstrated anti-inflammatory benefit; low saturated fat at approximately 1.0 g per 100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory gingerols in ginger; however, they should limit rice portion size to reduce inflammatory glycaemic load and request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger Sauce Chicken Rice, known in Vietnamese as Cơm Gà Kho Gừng (romanized: Com Ga Kho Gung), is a Vietnamese-origin rice dish widely served in Singaporean hawker centres and Vietnamese eateries across Southeast Asia. The dish consists of steamed white jasmine rice topped with chicken pieces that have been braised or caramelised in a fragrant sauce made from fresh ginger, fish sauce, soy sauce, garlic, sugar, and black pepper. The cooking technique 'kho' refers to a Vietnamese method of slow-braising protein in a sweet-savoury caramel sauce until the liquid reduces and coats the meat. The result is tender, deeply flavoured chicken with a sticky, amber-coloured ginger sauce that permeates the rice. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet, and warmly spiced with ginger and pepper. Nutritionally, the dish provides 116 kcal per 100 g, with 4.96 g protein, 4.0 g fat, and 15.12 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 324 mg per 100 g due to fish sauce and soy sauce. Ginger contributes anti-inflammatory gingerols. The dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions but requires sodium and carbohydrate management for individuals with hypertension or type 2 diabetes. A typical hawker serving weighs approximately 350 g, delivering around 406 kcal and 1,134 mg sodium.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010532) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The dish is an exact match in the HPB canonical database. GI is estimated from the analogous steamed white rice entry in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the closest approved GI source for this composite dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this Vietnamese-Singaporean braised chicken rice dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry G00010532 as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g is estimated at 1.0 g per 100 g based on the known fat composition of braised chicken with skin-off preparation and white rice, consistent with HPB compositional guidance for similar chicken rice dishes; this is an approximation as the HPB record does not explicitly report saturated fat. GI of 68 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white jasmine rice per the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for Com Ga Kho Gung as a composite dish. This dish is classified as a Singaporean dish for multilingual naming purposes given its HPB provenance and SG region tag; Vietnamese naming is also included given the Vietnamese culinary origin of the dish.
